---
title: "08 · Quaternions and 3D Rotations"
subtitle: "Hamilton's invention — the right tool for rotating things smoothly in 3D."
lesson-id: "t7.quaternions"
tier: 7
prerequisites: ["t6.groups", "t5.linear-algebra-foundation"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T6 L02 — Groups and Symmetry](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/02-groups/index.qmd) and [T5 L02 — Linear Algebra Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/02-linear-algebra-foundation/index.qmd)?
2. Recall: complex numbers $a + bi$, matrix multiplication, dot/cross products.
3. Have you encountered Euler angles or rotation matrices?
:::

## Touch

Rotate a 3D object smoothly from orientation A to orientation B.

```{ojs}
viewof rotMethod = Inputs.select(["Euler angles (gimbal lock!)", "Rotation matrix (overdetermined)", "Quaternion (smooth)"], {value: "Quaternion (smooth)", label: "Method:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info, count;
  switch(rotMethod) {
    case "Euler angles (gimbal lock!)": info = "3 numbers (roll, pitch, yaw). Suffers gimbal lock — singularity at certain orientations."; count = "3 params"; break;
    case "Rotation matrix (overdetermined)": info = "9 entries with 6 constraints (orthogonality + det = 1). 3 effective DOF but overdetermined storage."; count = "9 nums, 3 DOF"; break;
    case "Quaternion (smooth)": info = "4 numbers (w, x, y, z), |q| = 1. No gimbal lock. Smooth interpolation via slerp."; count = "4 nums, 3 DOF"; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.1rem; color:#1a237e;">${count}</div>
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95rem; color:#444;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Quaternions: invented Oct 16, 1843 by Hamilton on Brougham Bridge.
**The standard rotation representation in modern game engines.**

## Wonder

1. Complex numbers $a + bi$ encode 2D rotations. Could there be a 4D
   number system encoding 3D rotations?
2. **Quaternions** $\mathbb{H}$: $a + bi + cj + dk$ with $i^2 = j^2 =
   k^2 = ijk = -1$.
3. Multiplication is *non-commutative* — exactly what's needed for 3D
   rotation (which is also non-commutative).

## Discover

### Problem 1

Define $\mathbb{H} = \{a + bi + cj + dk : a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{R}\}$
with $i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1$.

Derive $ij$ and $ji$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
From $ijk = -1$ multiply right by $k^{-1} = -k$: $ij = -1 \cdot (-k) =
k$. Similarly $jk = i$, $ki = j$.

For $ji$: from $ij = k$, multiply left by $j^{-1} = -j$: $i = -jk$, so
$ji = -k$.

So **$ij = -ji = k$, $jk = -kj = i$, $ki = -ik = j$.** Anti-commutative
on imaginaries — like cross product.

This non-commutativity is what makes $\mathbb{H}$ encode 3D rotations.
</details>

### Problem 2

For $q = a + bi + cj + dk$, define **conjugate** $\bar q = a - bi - cj
- dk$ and **norm** $|q| = \sqrt{q \bar q}$.

Show $|pq| = |p||q|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|pq|^2 = (pq)(\overline{pq}) = pq \bar q \bar p = p (q \bar q) \bar
p = p |q|^2 \bar p = |q|^2 p \bar p = |q|^2 |p|^2$.

(We used $\overline{pq} = \bar q \bar p$ — order reverses!)

So $|pq| = |p| |q|$. Multiplicativity. (Hence non-zero quaternions
form a group under multiplication.)
</details>

### Problem 3

A **unit quaternion** has $|q| = 1$. Set
$q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2)(u_1 i + u_2 j + u_3 k)$
where $\hat{u} = (u_1, u_2, u_3)$ is a unit vector.

Define rotation: for a 3D vector $\vec v = v_1 i + v_2 j + v_3 k$,
$$
R_q(\vec v) = q \vec v \bar q.
$$
Verify this is a rotation by angle $\theta$ around axis $\hat u$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try $\hat u = (1, 0, 0)$, so $q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2) i$.
Compute $q (v_2 j + v_3 k) \bar q$ — should rotate the $jk$-plane.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes** — a careful computation shows $R_q$ rotates $\vec v$ by angle
$\theta$ around axis $\hat u$.

Verify: $R_q(\hat u) = \hat u$ (axis is fixed); $R_q$ preserves length;
the rotation in the plane orthogonal to $\hat u$ has the right angle.

Note: $q$ and $-q$ give the same rotation. So unit quaternions are a
**double cover** of $SO(3)$: $S^3 \to SO(3)$, kernel $\{\pm 1\}$.
</details>

### Problem 4

**Composition of rotations.** $R_{q_2} \circ R_{q_1} = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$R_{q_2}(R_{q_1}(\vec v)) = q_2 (q_1 \vec v \bar q_1) \bar q_2 = (q_2
q_1) \vec v \overline{(q_2 q_1)} = R_{q_2 q_1}(\vec v)$.

So **composition = quaternion product**. To combine rotations, just
multiply the quaternions.

Compare to rotation matrices: also composition by multiplication, but
9 entries vs 4 — quaternions are more compact and avoid singularities.
</details>

### Problem 5

**SLERP** (spherical linear interpolation). For unit quaternions $q_0,
q_1$, define
$$
\text{slerp}(q_0, q_1, t) = \frac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega} q_0 + \frac{\sin(t \Omega)}{\sin\Omega} q_1
$$
where $\cos\Omega = q_0 \cdot q_1$.

Why is slerp the *right* interpolation for rotations?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Slerp traces a geodesic on $S^3$** (the unit-quaternion sphere) at
constant angular velocity.

Naive linear interpolation $((1-t)q_0 + t q_1)$ gives non-unit
quaternions and uneven angular speed.

Slerp gives:

- Smooth motion.
- Constant angular velocity.
- Shortest path through orientation space.

Used in: every modern animation system, character rigging, IK solvers.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Quaternions** $\mathbb{H} = \{a + bi + cj + dk\}$ with $i^2 = j^2 =
k^2 = ijk = -1$.

**Multiplication:** non-commutative. $ij = k$, $jk = i$, $ki = j$.

**Conjugate** $\bar q = a - bi - cj - dk$. **Norm** $|q|^2 = q\bar q$.
$\overline{pq} = \bar q \bar p$.

**Inverse** of unit $q$: $q^{-1} = \bar q$.

**Unit quaternions** $\cong S^3$ form a group, double-covering $SO(3)$.

**Rotation by $\theta$ around unit axis $\hat u$:**
$$
q = \cos(\theta/2) + \sin(\theta/2) (u_1 i + u_2 j + u_3 k); \qquad R_q(\vec v) = q \vec v \bar q.
$$

**Composition:** $R_{q_2} \circ R_{q_1} = R_{q_2 q_1}$.

**SLERP** for smooth rotation interpolation. **Geodesic on $S^3$.**

**Advantages over Euler angles:** no gimbal lock. Over rotation
matrices: more compact (4 vs 9 numbers); cheaper composition.

:::

> *Hamilton's i, j, k satisfy $i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1$;*
> *Unit quaternions $\cong S^3$ double-cover $SO(3)$;*
> *Rotate by $q\vec v\bar q$, compose by multiplication, interpolate by slerp.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> **Rotate $(1, 0, 0)$ by 90° around the $z$-axis.**

$\hat u = (0, 0, 1)$, $\theta = 90°$. So
$$
q = \cos 45° + \sin 45° \cdot k = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1 + k).
$$

$\vec v = i$, $\bar q = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1 - k)$.

$q \vec v = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1 + k) i = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(i + ki) =
\frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(i + j)$.

$(q \vec v) \bar q = \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(i + j) \cdot \frac{\sqrt 2}{2}(1
- k) = \frac{1}{2}(i - ik + j - jk) = \frac{1}{2}(i + j + j - i) = j$.

So $i \mapsto j$ — exactly a 90° rotation around $z$. ✓

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $(1 + i)(1 + j)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 + j + i + ij = 1 + i + j + k$.
</details>

**W2.** What's $|2 + i + j + k|$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{4 + 1 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{7}$.
</details>

**W3.** What rotation does $q = i$ encode?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$q = i = \cos 90° + \sin 90° \cdot i$, so 180° rotation around $x$-axis.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Why are quaternions a **division ring** but not a field?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Every non-zero quaternion has an inverse: $q^{-1} = \bar q / |q|^2$.

But multiplication is **non-commutative** ($ij \neq ji$), so $\mathbb{H}$
is not a *field*. It's a *division ring* (or "skew field").

Frobenius: the only finite-dimensional associative division algebras
over $\mathbb{R}$ are $\mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{H}$.
</details>

**P2.** Why is the relation between quaternions and $SO(3)$ a *double*
cover?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both $q$ and $-q$ produce the same rotation (since $(-q)\vec v(-\bar q)
= q\vec v \bar q$).

Topologically: $S^3$ (unit quaternions) is simply connected; $SO(3)$
is not. The map $q \mapsto R_q$ is 2-to-1.

Consequence: a 360° rotation in $SO(3)$ corresponds to going from $1$
to $-1$ in $S^3$ — *not* back to identity. 720° gets back. ("Belt
trick" / "Dirac string".)
</details>

**P3.** Why does the **NLERP** (normalised linear interpolation) often
substitute for slerp in games?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
NLERP: $((1-t)q_0 + t q_1)/|...|$ — linear then renormalise.

Faster than slerp (no trig). Doesn't have constant angular velocity,
but the visual difference is tiny for small angles.

Trade-off: speed vs angular-velocity smoothness. Most games use NLERP
when frame-rate matters more than physical correctness.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Octonions** $\mathbb{O}$: 8-dim, non-associative.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
After $\mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{H}$, the octonions $\mathbb{O}$
are 8-dim with multiplicative norm — but **non-associative**: $(ab)c \neq
a(bc)$ in general.

Hurwitz: only normed division algebras are $\mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C},
\mathbb{H}, \mathbb{O}$ (dimensions 1, 2, 4, 8).

Used in: exceptional Lie groups, string theory, certain finite simple
groups.
</details>

**S2.** **Dual quaternions** for rigid-body motion (rotation +
translation).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Dual quaternion: $\hat q = q + \epsilon p$ where $\epsilon^2 = 0$.

Encodes both rotation ($q$) and translation ($p$) compactly.
Composition is just dual-quaternion multiplication. Interpolation:
"DLB" (dual quaternion linear blending) for character skinning.

Used in robotics, character animation. Avoids "candy wrapper" artefacts
of separate rotation + translation interpolation.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Quaternions are 4D rotations."** No — they encode 3D rotations
   (using 4 numbers). Genuine 4D rotations need a different setup.
2. **"$q\vec v \bar q$ vs $\bar q \vec v q$."** Both rotate, but in
   opposite directions (one is the inverse of the other). Pick a
   convention and stick to it.
3. **"Half-angle is a hack."** It's deep — ties to the double cover
   $S^3 \to SO(3)$. The half is *necessary*.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Define quaternion multiplication ($ij = ?$, $ji = ?$).
2. Why are unit quaternions a double cover of $SO(3)$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems?

(Answer: $ij = k$, $ji = -k$. $\pm q$ map to same rotation.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[09 · Relational Algebra](../09-relational-algebra/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **P2** (double cover).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T6 L02 — Groups and Symmetry](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/02-groups/index.qmd)**
- **[T5 L02 — Linear Algebra Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/02-linear-algebra-foundation/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ and spin physics; geometric algebra; Clifford
  algebras.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Standard rotation representation in Unity, Unreal,
Godot. SLERP is everywhere.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Robotics.** Pose representation. Trajectory planning on $SO(3)$.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Computer graphics.** Animation, IK, skinning. Avoids gimbal lock.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics.** Spin-1/2 particles. The half-angle ↔ $S^3 \to SO(3)$
double cover ↔ spinors.
:::
